intitule: National Symbols and Identity: Vocabulary Recognition

enonce.texte:
Exercise 1: Match the word to its definition.
Draw a line to connect each term on the left with the correct meaning on the right.

sousQuestions.reponse: c. A piece of cloth with symbols representing a country.
e. A short sentence expressing the beliefs of a country.
a. The ability to speak two languages officially.
b. Having many different types of people, cultures, or languages.
d. A community sharing the same language, culture, or history.

intitule: Fill in the Blanks with Key Vocabulary

enonce.texte:
Exercise 2: Complete the sentences.
Use the words from the box below. Each word is used only once.

Words: anthem, coat of arms, symbol, tribe, official language, integration

questions.texte: Fill in each blank with the correct word.
questions.reponse: 2.1 symbol, 2.2 coat of arms, 2.3 anthem, 2.4 official language, 2.5 tribe, 2.6 integration

sousQuestions.texte: The lion is often a _______ of strength and courage for many nations.
Cameroon's _______ features a shield, two crossed fasces, and a scale of justice.
Citizens stand and sing the national _______ during official ceremonies.
French and English are the two _______ in Cameroon.
A _______ is a smaller group within a country that shares the same customs.
National unity requires the _______ of all ethnic groups into one society.

intitule: Understanding the Cameroonian Motto and Flag

enonce.texte:
Exercise 3: Analyze and explain.
Read the information and answer the questions.

The Cameroonian flag has three vertical stripes: green, red, and yellow. The green represents the forest and hope. The red represents unity and independence. The yellow represents the sun and savannah. The national motto is "Peace, Work, Fatherland".
images: Vertical tricolor flag of Cameroon: green stripe on the left, red stripe in the center, yellow stripe on the right.

questions.texte: Analyze the meaning of the Cameroonian flag and motto.

sousQuestions.texte: Identify which color on the flag symbolizes the unity of all Cameroonians.
Explain why the color green is important for Cameroon's economy and environment.
sousQuestions.reponse: Green represents the forest, which provides timber, biodiversity, and supports agriculture (like cocoa and coffee). It also symbolizes hope for the future.

sousQuestions.texte: Evaluate the importance of having a national motto. Why is 'Fatherland' (the country) mentioned after 'Peace' and 'Work'?
sousQuestions.reponse: A national motto unites citizens around shared values. 'Fatherland' comes last because without peace and hard work, the country cannot be strong. Peace and work are the foundations for a prosperous nation.

enonce.texte:
Exercise 4: Write a short speech.
You are the class delegate. Your school has students from over 10 different ethnic groups (Bamileke, Beti, Bassa, Fulani, etc.). Some students refuse to sit with those from other tribes. Write a 100-150 word speech to promote tolerance and national identity.

questions.texte: Write a speech using at least five vocabulary words from the lesson (diversity, tolerance, integration, symbol, respect, etc.).
questions.reponse: Model answer: 'Dear fellow students, our school is a symbol of Cameroon's great diversity. We come from different ethnic groups, but we share one national identity. Tolerance means accepting that our classmates may eat different food or speak a different first language. Integration happens when we sit together, study together, and play together. Disrespecting someone because of their tribe is an insult to our national motto of peace. Let us show respect for our elders' advice: unity is strength. Today, promise to make one friend from a different tribe. Together, we build one fatherland.'

enonce.texte:
Exercise 5: Sort the items.
Place each item in the correct column: 'Official National Symbol' or 'Cultural/Traditional Symbol'.

Items: National Anthem, Ndop cloth, Coat of Arms, National Flag, Douala dance, Makossa music, Motto, Mount Cameroon (as a landmark).

questions.reponse: Official: National Anthem, Coat of Arms, National Flag, Motto. Cultural: Ndop cloth, Douala dance, Makossa music, Mount Cameroon.

intitule: Real-Life Problem Solving: Bilingualism in Cameroon

enonce.texte:
Exercise 6: Case study.
You are working in a government office in Yaoundé. An English-speaking citizen comes to ask for help, but the receptionist (who only speaks French) ignores him. The citizen gets angry and says, 'Cameroon is a bilingual country!'

questions.texte: Analyze the situation and propose a solution.

sousQuestions.texte: Identify the problem in this situation.
sousQuestions.reponse: The receptionist is not respecting Cameroon's official bilingualism, which excludes the English-speaking citizen from receiving service.

sousQuestions.texte: Propose two practical solutions to prevent this problem in the future.
sousQuestions.reponse: 1) Hire bilingual receptionists or train staff in basic English. 2) Install a sign indicating that citizens have the right to be served in either official language. 3) Create a translation hotline within the office.

sousQuestions.texte: Justify why bilingualism is a key part of Cameroon's national identity.
sousQuestions.reponse: Bilingualism reflects the historical union of French Cameroon and British Cameroon. It is a symbol of national integration and prevents division. It allows citizens from both regions to participate in government, education, and work. Rejecting bilingualism weakens national unity and contradicts the constitution.
